title: The Deconstruction of Christianity, Part 1
speaker: Elder Nick Anderson
source_files: 1-19-25 - The Deconstruction of Christianity - Part 1 - Elder Nick Anderson.txt

outline.title: Apostasy Is Not New
subpoints: Demas forsook Paul 'having loved this present world' (2 Timothy 4:10)
Scripture repeatedly warns of people departing from the faith
Deconstruction is the contemporary name for the ancient problem of apostasy
Examples from Scripture: the wilderness generation, false prophets, false teachers

outline.title: What Is Deconstruction?
subpoints: Term coined by 20th-century French philosopher Jacques Derrida
Derrida defined it as dismantling excessive loyalty to any idea to find truth in its opposite
Applied to faith, it becomes a postmodern process of rethinking faith without Scripture as standard
Distinguished from legitimate doubt: deconstruction disregards Scripture entirely
Notable examples: Kevin Max, Joshua Harris, Derek Webb, Paul Maxwell

outline.title: Three Core Elements of Deconstruction
subpoints: Not concerned with correct theology; focused on tearing down doctrines that seem morally wrong
No defined end point: 'there is no right way to deconstruct nor a right destination'
No external authority: the self is the sole authority
The locus of authority shifts from the Bible to the self

outline.title: Where Does Deconstruction Lead?
subpoints: Outcomes vary: atheism, agnosticism, progressive Christianity, secular humanism
The only common thread is rejection of the Bible
It is not a cohesive movement but a collection of individual journeys
Deconstruction effectively has no end since each person determines their own destination

outline.title: Five Reasons People Are Leaving Evangelicalism
subpoints: A literal reading of the Bible (caricature: the Bible communicates literal truth but not always in a literal way)
Belief that women are to be submissive to men (Bible teaches wives submit to husbands, not all women to all men)
Sanctity of heterosexuality and rejection of homosexuality (accurately represents biblical teaching)
Assumption that the American way of life is best (Bible is not tied to any nation's rise or fall)
Identification and partnership with political conservatism (Christian politics flows from theology)

summary:
Elder Nick Anderson opens a five-part Sunday school series based on the book 'The Deconstruction of Christianity' by Alisa Childers and Tim Barnett. He establishes that deconstruction is not a new phenomenon but the latest form of the ancient problem of apostasy, tracing it through Scripture from the wilderness generation to the New Testament warnings in 1 Timothy, Hebrews, and 2 Peter. The term itself was coined by 20th-century French philosopher Jacques Derrida, but the underlying impulse to abandon revealed truth for self-authority goes back to the Garden of Eden.

Anderson defines faith deconstruction as 'a postmodern process of rethinking your faith without regarding Scripture as a standard,' distinguishing it from healthy doubt or theological wrestling. He identifies three core elements: deconstruction is not concerned with correct theology but with rejecting morally uncomfortable doctrines; it has no defined end point; and it recognizes no external authority, making the self the final arbiter of truth.

The lesson surveys notable figures who have publicly deconstructed their faith (Joshua Harris, Derek Webb, Paul Maxwell, Kevin Max) and discusses where deconstruction leads: atheism, agnosticism, progressive Christianity, or secular humanism. The common denominator is rejection of Scripture's authority.

Anderson then examines the five reasons Blake Chastain and the hashtag #exvangelical movement cite for leaving evangelicalism: a literal reading of the Bible, women's submission, heterosexuality, American nationalism, and political conservatism. He evaluates each, noting that some are caricatures of actual Christian teaching, some reflect genuine church failures, and some (such as the sanctity of heterosexual marriage) accurately represent what Scripture teaches and cannot be surrendered.
transcript:
## Introduction and Opening Prayer

For our adult Sunday school, we're starting a new series and the series is based on this book called the Deconstruction of Christianity. What it is, why it's destructive and how to respond. It's a good book and it's a timely book. Deconstruction, how many of you actually have heard the word Deconstruction? A lot of you have heard it. How many of you have a pretty good understanding of what it is. Okay, so this will be profitable for all of us, I think. Before I get into it, though, let's have a word of prayer. I wish this God, we thank you for your goodness to us and your faithfulness. We thank you that we can learn about your word through the books that people have written. We thank you that you are good to us in that and that you've given us great resources to learn from. Pray, Lord, that you would be with us now as we talk about this topic. We pray that you would help me speak only what is true and this will be profitable for your people. We have to send Christ's name and then.

## Apostasy Is Not New

In Colossians 4, verse 14, Paul writes this. He says, Luke the beloved physician and Demas greet you and then in Philemon chapter 1, verse 24, he also lists Demas there as among those who he refers to as his fellow laborers. But then in 2 Timothy 4, 10, Demas is mentioned one last time and Paul writes this. He says, for Demas has forsaken me having loved this present world and has departed for Thessalonica. It's a very sad comment for Paul to make about Demas. He forsook Paul because he loved this present world and this is not new for Paul's day from the very beginning, from Eden to Calvary to our present day, apostasy from the gospel has happened and it will continue to happen unfortunately. And it happens for the same reason every time and it's that people love the world more than they love Christ.

The scriptures warn us about apostasy. First Timothy 4, one puts it rather bluntly. It says, now the spirit expressly says that in latter time some will depart from the faith, giving heed to deceiving spirits and doctrines of demons. The Apostle John mentions this as well in his first letter: they went out from us but they were not of us for if they had been of us, they would have continued with us but they went out that they might be made manifest that none of them were of us. And interestingly that comes right after his admonition to us to not love the world or the things in the world.

In Hebrews 3, verse 12, the writer there writes, beware brethren, lest there be in any of you an evil heart of unbelief in departing from the living God but exhort one another daily while it is called today, lest any of you be hardened through the deceitfulness of sin. And if you keep reading in Hebrews it warns us again several times in the book of Hebrews actually and it points out that the people who came out of Egypt, the ones who saw the plagues, who saw the Red Sea split, who saw all these miracles, they were the ones who apostatized and died there in the wilderness because of their rebellion.

And 2 Peter 2 verses 1 through 3, Peter writes, but there were also false prophets among the people even as there will be false teachers among you who will secretly bring in destructive heresies even denying the Lord who bought them and bring on themselves swift destruction and many will follow in their destructive ways because of whom the way of truth will be blasphemed. By covetousness they will exploit you with deceptive words for a long time their judgment has not been idle and their destruction does not slumber.

The term itself is new in one sense but what's happening is not new at all. The more commonly used word for this, the more theological word for it that I've already stated is apostasy and clearly we need to be warned. We need to be aware of what's going on so that we ourselves are not deceived.

## Notable Examples of Deconstruction

There are some names that you might recognize. If you're around my age you're probably into DC Talk at one point in your life. Kevin Max was one of the members of DC Talk and he has since left the faith. He uses the hashtag #exvangelical and said he's been deconstructing his faith for decades. He actually claims that he still follows the universal Christ, whatever that is.

If you were about my age, you probably came across or heard about at least the book I Kissed Dating Goodbye. It was extremely popular in evangelical circles and Joshua Harris, the author, left the faith and he divorced his wife after almost two decades of marriage and then a couple weeks later after they announced their divorce he said this: the information that was left out of our announcement is that I have undergone a massive shift in regard to my faith in Jesus. The popular phrase for this is deconstruction. The biblical phrase is falling away. By all the measures I have for defining a Christian, I am not a Christian.

This one is particularly painful for me. I was a big fan of Derek Webb's music — he was a member of the band Caedmon's Call and had a pretty successful solo career and he has completely become an enemy of the faith. Totally promoting the LGBTQ agenda.

Paul Maxwell was a theological writer for Desiring God and he says he's no longer a Christian. But it's not just celebrities who are deconstructing. It's ordinary people. It's people we know.

## What Is Deconstruction?

What exactly is deconstruction? The origin of this concept came from a 20th century French philosopher named Jacques Derrida. He coined the term and defined it as dismantling our excessive loyalty to any idea and learning to see the aspects of the truth that might be buried in its opposite. Now in and of itself this might not actually be a bad thing. We might have an excessive loyalty to something that we shouldn't have loyalty to. Examining that might actually be a good thing. But we're going to see how it has become a very destructive thing.

Derrida applied this to literary texts and political institutions but it's been applied basically by other people to every aspect of life. There are people in Christian circles who tried to take that word and turn it into a good thing, but it's very very difficult to actually do that. Deconstruction is rooted in postmodernism and it has a lot of baggage that comes with that term.

There are three core elements that go into this idea of deconstruction. The first core element is that deconstruction really is not concerned with correct theology. It's more concerned with tearing down the doctrines that appear morally wrong to you — and the 'you' part is probably the most important part of deconstruction. The main concern is being true to yourself. The second core element of deconstruction is that there really isn't an end. There is no set point where everyone agrees that you're done deconstructing. One proponent of deconstruction says there's no right way to deconstruct nor is there a right destination. You do you. The third core element is there's no external authority in deconstruction to tell you what your view of anything should look like. At the end of the day, in deconstruction, you are the sole authority. The locus of authority in someone deconstructing shifts from the Bible to the self. That brings us right back to where we started this whole mess, Genesis 2.

The definition we're going to use is: faith deconstruction is a postmodern process of rethinking your faith without regarding Scripture as a standard. When we talk about deconstruction, we're not talking about a person who is wrestling through their beliefs. We're not talking about a person who has some doubts and is going to the scriptures and trying to figure out what they should believe. Those are healthy things. What we're talking about is someone who is going through a process whereby they are completely disregarding the scriptures and trying to figure out what they believe without regard for what the Bible teaches.

## Where Does Deconstruction Lead?

If someone's going through this, where are they going to end up? Well, there's any number of places. Some become atheists. Others become agnostics. Some end up in progressive Christianity. Some will end up being secular humanists. In reality, deconstruction doesn't really end, because there is no defining end point. The only commonality it seems like is rejection of the Bible.

## Five Reasons People Are Leaving Evangelicalism

Why are people deconstructing their faith? One common thread seems to be that people are leaving evangelicalism. Karl Truman has pointed out that evangelical and evangelicalism are not well-defined terms but there seem to be at least four points that characterize someone who calls themselves evangelical: the Bible is the source of truth; Christ's atonement on the cross for our sins; personal conversion; and the faithful preaching of the gospel.

Ligonier's research reveals some disturbing statistics among self-identified evangelicals: 43% believe that Jesus was a great teacher but he was not God; 56% believe that God accepts the worship of all religions including Christianity, Judaism, and Islam; 38% believe that religious belief is a matter of personal opinion and not about objective truth.

Blake Chastain — the creator of the #exvangelical hashtag — set forth five points or reasons why people are leaving Christianity: a literal reading of the Bible; a belief that women are to be submissive to men; the sanctity of heterosexuality and the rejection of homosexuality; the assumption that the American way of life is best; and identification and partnership with political conservatism.

Regarding the literal reading of the Bible: we mean that the Bible communicates literal truth, but not always in a literal way. It communicates according to the literary style that is being used — historical narrative, poetry, law codes, wisdom literature, prophecy. A rejection of the literal reading of the Bible often means the Bible should not be relied upon for literal truth and can safely be ignored.

Regarding women and submission: the Bible does not teach that all women have to submit to all men. Wives are to be submissive to their husbands, and that submission is to be of the same character as our submission to Christ. The problem that deconstructionists have is that they view submission as inherently oppressive — but Christ submitted to the Father, which by their logic would mean he was oppressed.

Regarding heterosexuality: the Bible teaches that God created man and woman with no other genders, and that sex was created to be between one man and one woman within the context of marriage. This has been the teaching of the Catholic Church, Eastern Orthodox, and Protestants for millennia.

Regarding nationalism: the Bible was not written after the United States was formed. God's redemptive plan is not tied to any single nation.

Regarding political conservatism: for Christians, politics is informed by our theology. Our view of the nature of man as sinful and fallen makes limited government look good to us. Our belief that human life begins at conception shapes how we vote. Liberals tend to believe human nature is generally good or can be fundamentally changed apart from Christ; Christians rightly reject this idea.

Some of these points are caricatures of what the Bible teaches; some are definitely not what the Bible teaches; and some are exactly what the Bible teaches. The most troubling thing with people who are deconstructing is not that they are questioning their faith but that they are rejecting their faith altogether.

## Closing Prayer

Lord, we thank you for your faithfulness to us. We thank you that you are a good and gracious God who we can trust. We thank you that your word is indeed trustworthy, that it is a lamp to our feet. We thank you for it. We pray that you would help us love it and love Christ more and more each day. We ask this in Christ's name. Amen.
